chunk_summary: Therapeutic elemental iron dosing in suspected iron deficiency anemia (IDA) focuses on individualized dosing (2–6 mg/kg/day), dietary adjustments, and monitoring hemoglobin response in children aged 6–24 months to address micronutrient gaps effectively.
learning_objectives: Understand therapeutic elemental iron dosing guidelines for suspected IDA in children aged 6–24 months.
Learn practical administration strategies to optimize iron absorption and minimize side effects.
Recognize the importance of monitoring hemoglobin response and adherence in iron therapy.
Identify dietary factors that contribute to iron deficiency and strategies to correct them.
key_facts: Therapeutic elemental iron dosing for infants and toddlers is typically 3–6 mg/kg/day, with emerging evidence supporting lower doses (2–3 mg/kg/day) for fewer side effects.
Iron should be administered away from large milk feeds and paired with vitamin C sources to enhance absorption.
Dark stools, constipation, or nausea are common side effects of iron therapy; caregivers should be counseled on adherence.
Monitoring hemoglobin response is recommended after 3–4 weeks to assess improvement and adherence.
WHO supports preventive iron strategies in high-anemia-burden settings for children aged 6–23 months when diets lack iron fortification.
reference_quote: Therapeutic dosing: commonly used elemental iron dosing in infants and toddlers is 3–6 mg/kg/day, with dose choice individualized by anemia severity and tolerance. Emerging evidence supports that lower dosing (around 2–3 mg/kg/day) may achieve hematologic response with fewer gastrointestinal side effects in some children.
document_key_topics: Therapeutic iron dosing for IDA in children
Dietary strategies to prevent micronutrient gaps
Monitoring and adherence in iron therapy
Preventive iron supplementation in high-anemia-burden settings
